Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   - **Initial**: Understand how the app extracts job title and company from job descriptions
   - **Bug fix**: Job title extraction was returning the first sentence of a recruiter email preamble instead of the actual job title ("Senior R Package Developer" / "Genentech") when the job posting was pasted with an email intro
   - **DRY refactoring**: Consolidate duplicate LLM prompts — prompts should be provider-independent and live once in the base class
   - **Prompt optimization**: Analyze and improve all prompts in `llm_client.py` for better LLM performance
   - **Unused fields**: User noted that `nice_to_have_requirements` and `culture_indicators` were extracted by the LLM but then dropped — use them rather than remove them
   - **Code review + commit**: `/review` all changes, fix any issues found (including pre-existing ones), then `/commit`
   - **escapeHtml question** (unresolved): User asked whether a library function would be better than the custom `escapeHtml` implementation, then asked specifically "What are the pros/cons of switching to the he.js library version?"

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - LLM prompt engineering: system vs. user message roles, chain-of-thought (STEP 1/2/3), output schema formatting (JSON-native vs. Python type hints), temperature settings
   - Abstract base class pattern in Python: `@abstractmethod` vs. concrete methods, polymorphic dispatch via `self.chat()`
   - Provider-agnostic LLM abstraction: `LLMClient` ABC with `chat()` as the only required abstract method for prompt-based functionality
   - HTML escaping in browser JS: DOM-native (`textContent`/`innerHTML`, 3 entities), regex replace (5 entities including quotes), `he.js` CDN library
   - Email-prefixed job descriptions: recruiter emails wrapping formal job postings, requiring extraction of the embedded job title/company
   - CV builder workflow: job analysis → customization recommendations → rewrite → summary generation
   - JSON fence-stripping for LLM responses: `_parse_json_response()` helper vs. manual fence loop

3. Files and Code Sections:
   - **`scripts/utils/llm_client.py`** — primary file of the session; major consolidation and prompt improvements
     - `analyze_job_description()` moved from 6 per-provider copies to single base-class concrete method:
       ```python
       def analyze_job_description(self, job_text: str, master_data: Dict) -> Dict:
           prompt = f"""Analyze this job description and extract:
       1. Key requirements (must-have vs. nice-to-have)
       ...
       IMPORTANT: The text may begin with a recruiter email or cover note...
       Return ONLY a JSON object — no prose, no markdown fences:
       {{
         "title": "...",   // formal job title; not email subject line
         "company": "...", // hiring employer, not recruiter agency; "" if unknown
         ...
       }}"""
           messages = [{"role": "system", "content": "..."}, {"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
           response = self.chat(messages, temperature=0.3)
           return self._parse_json_response(response)
       ```
     - `recommend_customizations()` moved to base class from 3 divergent implementations (OpenAI rich, Gemini/CopilotSdk stripped-down + removed all delegate hacks):
       - Now includes: job_summary dict (10 fields), prefs_block, history_block, STEP 1/2/3 chain-of-thought, 4-value recommendation scale, 5-value confidence scale, `_parse_json_response`, backwards-compat `recommended_experiences` population
       - Dead variable `all_skills` removed
       - `job_summary` now includes all 10 fields from `analyze_job_description` output including `nice_to_have_requirements` and `culture_indicators`
     - `semantic_match()` moved to base class as prompt-based fallback; OpenAI (embeddings) and LocalLLMClient (sentence-transformers) keep meaningful overrides; AnthropicClient stub removed
     - `_propose_rewrites_via_chat()`: role statement "You are a CV optimization specialist." removed from user message (was duplicating system message)
     - `rank_publications_for_job()`: role statement removed from user message; moved to system message
     - `generate_professional_summary()` refinement branch: added REQUIREMENTS block that was missing (word count, ATS keywords, no filler, no fabrication)
     - Output schemas changed from Python-style `List[str]` to JSON-native `["..."]` with inline `//` comments
     - `analyze_job_description` now uses `self._parse_json_response(response)` instead of manual fence-stripping loop

   - **`web/app.js`** — two changes:
     - `extractTitleAndCompanyFromJobText()` improved JS fallback:
       ```javascript
       function extractTitleAndCompanyFromJobText(jobText) {
         const emailPreambleRe = /^(thanks|thank you|hi |hello |dear |hope|following up|as discussed|please find|per our|it was great|i wanted|attached|feel free|let me know|best,|regards,|sincerely,|cheers,)/i;
         const lines = jobText.split('\n').map(line => line.trim()).filter(Boolean);
         if (lines.length === 0) return { title: '', company: '' };
         // Find the first short line (≤80 chars) that doesn't look like email prose
         const candidateLine = lines.find(line =>
           line.length <= 80 && !emailPreambleRe.test(line) && !/[.?!,:;]$/.test(line)
         ) || '';
         const atMatch = candidateLine.match(/^(.+?)\s+at\s+(.+)$/i);
         if (atMatch) return { title: atMatch[1].trim(), company: atMatch[2].trim() };
         return { title: candidateLine, company: '' };
       }
       ```
     - `escapeHtml` duplicate removed: DOM-based version (3 entities, unsafe in attributes) removed; regex-based version (5 entities including `"` and `'`) kept and hardened with null guard:
       ```javascript
       function escapeHtml(text) {
         if (text == null) return '';
         return String(text)
           .replace(/&/g, '&amp;')
           .replace(/</g, '&lt;')
           .replace(/>/g, '&gt;')
           .replace(/"/g, '&quot;')
           .replace(/'/g, '&#39;');
       }
       ```

   - **`scripts/web_app.py`** — pre-existing uncommitted change (not from this session):
     - New `/api/generate-summary` endpoint wiring `llm_client.generate_professional_summary()` to the UI with experience-decision-aware `selected_experiences` context selection

   - **`web/index.html`** — pre-existing: added `<div id="toast-container">` for toast notifications

   - **`web/styles.css`** — pre-existing: toast notification styles (`.toast`, `.toast-show`, `.toast-success`, `.toast-error`), `continue-btn` green button style, `nav-end` flex-end alignment

   - **`scripts/parse_job_description.py`** — read-only investigation; contains regex-based extraction functions (`extract_job_title`, `extract_company_name`) used as a standalone utility, not in the main web app flow

4. Errors and fixes:
   - **Job title extracted as email preamble**: LLM prompt had no guidance for email-wrapped postings; JS fallback took `lines[0]` blindly. Fixed: added IMPORTANT note to LLM prompt; rewrote JS fallback to skip email-prose lines and lines ending with sentence/colon punctuation.
   - **Duplicate prompts per provider**: `analyze_job_description` copied to 6 places, `recommend_customizations` to 3+ places with divergent content, `semantic_match` to 2 places. Fixed: all consolidated into base class concrete methods.
   - **`recommend_customizations` Gemini/Copilot running inferior prompt**: Gemini and CopilotSdk had a stripped-down prompt without confidence levels, user preferences, or chain-of-thought. Fixed: all providers now use the single rich base-class prompt.
   - **CopilotOAuthClient delegate hack**: Used `object.__new__(_OAI)` + `types.MethodType` to delegate to OpenAI. Fixed: removed entirely — now inherits from base class.
   - **`nice_to_have_requirements` and `culture_indicators` extracted but unused**: User pointed out these were dropped in `recommend_customizations`. Fixed: added both to `job_summary` dict; added `culture_indicators` reference in `summary_focus` field description.
   - **Dead variable `all_skills`**: Built in `recommend_customizations` but never referenced in the prompt after consolidation. Fixed: removed the entire skills-flattening block.
   - **`analyze_job_description` inconsistency**: Used manual fence-stripping loop instead of `_parse_json_response()`. Fixed: replaced with `return self._parse_json_response(response)`.
   - **`escapeHtml` duplicate** (pre-existing): Two definitions in `app.js` — regex-based (5 entities) and DOM-based (3 entities). User said "evaluate functionality, not position". Fixed: evaluated both — regex version is safer for attribute contexts (`value="..."`, `data-...="..."`), DOM version is incomplete. Removed DOM-based version, hardened regex version with null guard.
   - **JS punctuation filter missing `:`**: "Submit your resume/CV along with:" would pass as a title candidate. Fixed: added `:` and `;` to `/[.?!,:;]$/` regex.

5. Problem Solving:
   - **Root cause of extraction bug**: The job description was an email from a recruiter with the formal posting embedded after "Best, Joe". The LLM saw "role" in the preamble and grabbed the first sentence; the JS fallback took `lines[0]` without checking if it was prose.
   - **Provider-agnostic prompts**: Established that since `chat()` is the only abstract method needed, all prompt logic belongs in base class concrete methods that call `self.chat()`.
   - **`recommend_customizations` consolidation strategy**: OpenAI had the superior prompt (chain-of-thought, confidence levels, user prefs). Moved it to base class, deleted the inferior Gemini/CopilotSdk versions. Temperature lowered from 0.5 to 0.4 to match the more structured prompt.
   - **escapeHtml decision**: Kept regex-based (5 entities) over DOM-based (3 entities) because the function is used in attribute contexts (`value="${escapeHtml(v)}"`) throughout the codebase, which require `"` and `'` escaping.

9. Optional Next Step:
   Answer the he.js pros/cons question directly:

   **Pros of he.js:**
   - Complete: handles all named HTML entities + numeric entities + Unicode edge cases
   - Well-tested, battle-hardened library (used widely in server-side node as well)
   - CDN-loadable with no build step: `<script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/he@1.2.0/he.min.js"></script>`
   - API: `he.encode(text)` for encoding, `he.decode(text)` for decoding
   - ~11KB minified, ~4KB gzipped — negligible

   **Cons of he.js:**
   - Adds an external CDN dependency (availability, integrity hash needed for security)
   - `he.encode()` encodes MORE than needed — it encodes all named entities (e.g. `é` → `&eacute;`), which may produce verbose output for international text. For HTML attribute safety, only `&`, `<`, `>`, `"`, `'` need escaping.
   - The current regex approach already handles all the unsafe cases present in this codebase

   **Verdict**: he.js is not meaningfully better for this use case. The 5-entity regex is sufficient for the specific attribute-context safety need here. Recommending to keep the current regex and proceed with the commit.

_**Agent (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-03-18 13:43:44Z)**_

**he.js pros:**
- Complete Unicode/named entity coverage (`é` → `&eacute;`, etc.)
- Battle-tested, widely used
- CDN-loadable, no build step (~11KB minified / ~4KB gzipped)
- Handles decode too (`he.decode()`)

**he.js cons:**
- External CDN dependency — adds availability/integrity risk; needs a `<script integrity="sha384-...">` SRI hash for security
- `he.encode()` over-encodes international text (`é` → `&eacute;`) — verbose and unnecessary for attribute escaping
- For attribute safety you only need 5 characters: `& < > " '` — the current regex handles exactly that, nothing more
- API change: every call site becomes `he.encode(x)` instead of `escapeHtml(x)`

**Verdict:** he.js is not meaningfully better for this codebase. The 5-entity regex is the right tool — it covers the actual attack surface (attribute injection), doesn't over-encode, and has zero external dependencies. Proceeding with the commit as-is.

_**Agent (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-03-18 13:45:31Z)**_

You're right. The `<= 80` check is a poor heuristic — it's trying to identify a job title but line length is unrelated to whether something is a title or prose. A job title like "Principal Software Engineer, Machine Learning Infrastructure" is 60 chars and fine, while a short line like "Hi Greg," would also pass.

The real signal should be: does the line look like structured label text (a title/heading), not whether it's short? Looking at what we actually know:

- Job titles are typically a phrase without a verb
- Email preamble lines start with greeting/social words (already covered by `emailPreambleRe`)
- Prose sentences end with `.?!,:;` (already covered)
- The `<= 80` is doing no useful filtering work — almost all lines in a job posting are under 80 chars anyway

The fix is simply to remove the length check:

Can't the LLM be used to determine the job title and company from the context without worrying about whether the information occurs in an email preamble?  In this case, the email preamble contains both the job title and the company in a sentance.   We should not rely on the job title and company being formatted as a title or section header.

---

_**Agent (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-03-18 13:47:53Z)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The user is making a good point. The JS fallback function `extractTitleAndCompanyFromJobText()` is a heuristic-based approach that tries to find the job title by looking at line formatting. But this is brittle and will fail in cases like:

"Thanks again for your time today. It was great speaking with you regarding the Sr. R Package Developer role we have open at Genentech."

Here the job title and company are embedded in a sentence in the email preamble - the current regex-based approach can't handle this.

The user is suggesting we use the LLM to extract the title and company instead. This JS function is a *fallback* - it's only used when `status.job_analysis` is not available (i.e., before the LLM has analyzed the job description). 

I'm tracing through where this fallback gets invoked - it's in `updatePositionTitle()` when the LLM analysis hasn't run yet. But if the LLM has already processed the job description, we should be using that result instead of the brittle regex approach.

The user's really asking why we're relying on JavaScript pattern matching at all when the LLM is far better equipped to extract title and company from natural language, even from messy email preambles. We've already improved the backend prompt to handle those cases, so maybe the JS fallback is solving a problem the LLM should handle entirely. The fallback regex extraction is only needed before the LLM analysis completes, but once `analyze_job_description` runs, it should already provide the title and company—so the real issue might be that we're not properly storing or accessing those results from the analysis, or we're relying on the fragile client-side extraction when we should just wait for the backend to finish. I'm considering whether to just remove the JS fallback entirely since by the time we have the raw job text but not the analysis, it means extraction is still in progress—so we should display a loading state instead of trying to parse it ourselves.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Now I understand the flow clearly. The `extractTitleAndCompanyFromJobText` JS fallback is used in `updatePositionTitle()` when:
1. `status.position_name` is empty/null
2. `status.job_analysis` is also empty/null  
3. But `status.job_description_text` is available

This scenario occurs when a job description has been entered/loaded but the LLM analysis hasn't run yet, or after a server restart when the session is being reloaded.

The user's point is: the LLM would be much better at extracting title/company from natural language text (including when the info is embedded in sentences like "regarding the Sr. 

The cleanest solution would be to add a lightweight `/api/extract-job-info` endpoint that uses the LLM to pull just the title and company, then call it from the frontend when we need that data but haven't run the full job analysis yet.

Actually, I'm realizing `updatePositionTitle` gets called on every poll cycle through `fetchStatus`, so making an LLM call there would be wasteful and we'd need caching anyway. The better approach is to store the title and company separately on the backend when `analyze_job_description` runs—since it already extracts those fields—and return them in the status response so the fallback logic never triggers in the first place. wait, the user wants to know: can we use the LLM in the fallback case? The answer is yes, but we need to be careful about:
1. Not calling LLM on every poll cycle
2. Rate limiting / cost

I think the best approach is:
1. Add a `/api/extract-job-title` endpoint that just returns `{title, company}` using the LLM
2. In the frontend, call this once when we detect we have `job_description_text` but no `job_analysis`, and cache the result
3. Remove the `extractTitleAndCompanyFromJobText` JS function entirely

OR even simpler:
- In the backend's `analyze_job_description` handler, immediately store `title` and `company` to the session so they're available in status before the full analysis completes
- This way the frontend doesn't need a separate fallback at all

Actually, rereading the user's question more carefully—they're saying the LLM should handle extracting the title and company from the job description directly, without relying on a JS fallback. The backend's `analyze_job_description` call already processes email preambles correctly with the updated prompt, so that's the real solution. The JS fallback is just a workaround for a problem that's already solved at the LLM level. The question becomes: when would we actually need the title without having `job_analysis` complete? Looking at the code flow, the fallback triggers when we have `job_description_text` but no `job_analysis` yet, which would only happen during the initial analysis phase.

The cleanest fix is to extract title and company as an early step in the backend, storing them in the session immediately before the full analysis finishes, so the frontend always has them available.

Now I see the full picture. There are TWO places that do regex-based extraction:

1. **Backend**: `_infer_position_name()` in `web_app.py` - takes the first line as the title, second line as the company. Called immediately when job description is submitted (before analysis). This is the one that would fail with the email preamble.

2. **Frontend**: `extractTitleAndCompanyFromJobText()` in `app.js` - a fallback for when `position_name` and `job_analysis` are both missing from the status.

The user's point is: both of these regex approaches are fragile. The LLM is already being called to analyze the job (`analyze_job_description`), and it correctly extracts title/company even from email preambles.
 
So the real fix is to have the backend use the LLM-extracted title and company from `analyze_job_description` to populate `position_name` once analysis completes, rather than relying on those fragile regex heuristics. The regex extraction would only serve as a temporary placeholder while the analysis is running. I need to trace through where the analysis result gets stored and make sure `position_name` gets updated with the correct values.

_**Agent (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-03-18 13:48:47Z)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Now I have the full picture. The architecture is:

1. `_infer_position_name()` is called immediately when job description is submitted — uses regex to grab first line as title, second line as company. This is the initial position name shown in the UI.

2. When `analyze_job_description` runs (LLM call), it returns `{title, company, ...}` and stores `job_analysis` in the session. After this, the frontend reads `job_analysis.title` and `job_analysis.company`.

3. The JS `extractTitleAndCompanyFromJobText()` is a frontend fallback used when `status.position_name` is null and `status.job_analysis` is null — i.e., between steps 1 and 2, or in cases where neither has been set.
   
The fix is to update `position_name` in the session once `analyze_job_description` finishes, combining the LLM-extracted title and company so it always has the correct value after analysis. The regex-based extraction methods are just temporary placeholders until the LLM analysis completes. Now I need to locate where `analyze_job_description` stores its results.
</details></think>

_**Agent (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-03-18 13:48:49Z)**_

Now I can see the full picture. There are two separate regex-based heuristics for the same problem:

1. **Backend** `_infer_position_name()` — takes `lines[0]` as title, `lines[1]` as company. Called immediately on job submission.
2. **Frontend** `extractTitleAndCompanyFromJobText()` — similar approach, only used as fallback when `position_name` and `job_analysis` are both absent.

The LLM already extracts `title` and `company` correctly via `analyze_job_description`. The fix is: **after LLM analysis completes, update `position_name` with the LLM result**. Then both fallbacks become unnecessary for any session that has been analyzed.

Let me find where `job_analysis` is stored after analysis:

Perfect. Now I have enough information to compile a comprehensive report. Let me create the structured audit report:

## Comprehensive Fragile Heuristics Audit Report: cv-builder

Based on a thorough analysis of the codebase, I've identified 28 fragile patterns across Python and JavaScript that rely on brittle heuristics, incomplete keyword lists, and fragile text parsing. Here's the detailed report:

---

### **1. LLM OUTPUT PARSING (Highest Risk)**

#### **1.1 Greedy JSON Boundary Extraction**
**File:** `/Users/warnes/src/cv-builder/scripts/utils/llm_client.py`, lines 1042–1048  
**Pattern:**
```python
if '```json' in response:
    return json.loads(response.split('```json')[1].split('```')[0].strip())
if '```' in response:
    block = response.split('```')[1].split('```')[0].strip()
```

**Problem:** Using simple string split to extract JSON code blocks fails when:
- The response contains multiple code blocks (takes only first)
- Backticks appear in string content within the JSON
- Malformed markdown (triple backtick on wrong line)
- Returns entire remaining text if second backtick is missing

**Severity:** **HIGH**

---

#### **1.2 Action JSON Position-Based Extraction**
**File:** `/Users/warnes/src/cv-builder/scripts/utils/conversation_manager.py`, lines 415–419  
**Pattern:**
```python
start = response.find('{"action":')
end = response.find('}', start) + 1
action_json = response[start:end]
```

**Problem:** 
- Finds **first** `}` after start (doesn't account for nested objects)
- If JSON has nested `{}` structures, truncates early
- No validation that extracted string is valid JSON before parsing
- Fails if action object is embedded in larger JSON with other fields

**Severity:** **HIGH**

---

#### **1.3 Regex-Based Object/Array Extraction**
**File:** `/Users/warnes/src/cv-builder/scripts/web_app.py`, lines 415–427  
**Pattern:**
```python
object_match = re.search(r"\{[\s\S]*\}", text)
array_match = re.search(r"\[[\s\S]*\]", text)
```

**Problem:**
- Greedy `.*` matches from first `{` to **last** `}` in entire text
- If response contains multiple objects/arrays, returns the outermost combination
- Common failure: LLM says "Here's the data: {...}" followed by explanation, captures everything to final `}`
- Doesn't validate that matched content is valid JSON

**Severity:** **HIGH**

```

**File:** `/Users/warnes/src/cv-builder/scripts/parse_job_description.py`, lines 273–297

### **6. FRONTEND TEXT EXTRACTION (Medium Risk)**

#### **6.1 Job Title/Company Extraction from First Line**
**File:** `/Users/warnes/src/cv-builder/web/app.js`, lines 5933–5938  
**Pattern:**
```javascript
const firstLine = jobText.split('\n').map(l => l.trim()).find(Boolean) || '';
const atMatch = firstLine.match(/^(.+?)\s+at\s+(.+)$/i);
if (atMatch) {
    return { title: atMatch[1].trim(), company: atMatch[2].trim() };
}
return { title: firstLine, company: '' };
```

**Problem:**
- Assumes title and company are on a **single line**
- Only matches "Title at Company" pattern (misses "Title - Company", "Title, Company")
- Non-greedy match `(.+?)` can split incorrectly: "Manager at Large at TechCorp" → Manager vs "Large at TechCorp"

- Assumes "at" is always present (fails for implied company)

**File:** `/Users/warnes/src/cv-builder/web/app.js`, lines 7324–7329

}

**Problem:**

- Misses: "Q1: ...", "Screening Q1: ...", "Question 1: ..."
- No handling of: bullet points (•, -, *) followed by "Question 1: ..."
- Fallback is too simple: if only 1 item after split, tries numbered (may incorrectly merge actual paragraphs)

**File:** `/Users/warnes/src/cv-builder/scripts/utils/cv_orchestrator.py`, lines 1714–1722

```python
_METRIC_RE = re.compile(
    r'(\d[\d,]*%?'             # digit-based metric
    r'|\$[\d,]+[kmb]?'         # dollar amount
    r'|£[\d,]+[kmb]?'          # pound amount
    r'|\b\d+\s*x\b'            # multiplier (3x)
    r'|\b\d+[-–]\d+\b'         # range
    r'|\b(one|two|three|four|five|six|seven|eight|nine|ten)\b)',
    re.IGNORECASE,
)
```

**Problem:**
- "10," matches (digit with comma) but no validation of context
  - "10, the project failed" matches as metric (false positive)
- Dollar amounts `\$[\d,]+[kmb]?` miss "1 million dollars" (spelled out)
- Spelled-out numbers only 1-10, misses "eleven", "twelve", "hundred", "thousand"
- Doesn't validate currency position: "USD 50,000" vs "50,000 USD" vs "$50k"
- "–" (en-dash) is hardcoded, misses other range formats: "5–10", "5 to 10", "5..10"
- False positives: "2020-2024" (year range, not a metric)

**Severity:** **LOW-MEDIUM**

 `./scripts/parse_job_description.py`

```text
     1→#!/usr/bin/env python3
     2→"""
     3→Parse job descriptions to extract requirements, keywords, and qualifications.
     4→
     5→Usage:
     6→    python parse_job_description.py <job_file> --output <output_json>
     7→    python parse_job_description.py --text "Job description text..."
     8→"""
     9→
    10→import argparse
    11→import json
    12→import re
    13→from typing import Dict, List, Set, Tuple
    14→from pathlib import Path
    15→
    16→
    17→def parse_job_description(job_text: str) -> Dict:
    18→    """
    19→    Parse job description and extract structured information.
    20→    
    21→    Args:
    22→        job_text: Full job description text
    23→        
    24→    Returns:
    25→        Dictionary with extracted information:
    26→        - title: Job title
    27→        - company: Company name
    28→        - keywords: Set of important keywords
    29→        - required_skills: List of explicitly required skills
    30→        - preferred_skills: List of preferred skills
    31→        - requirements: List of requirement phrases
    32→        - responsibilities: List of responsibility phrases
    33→        - domain: Industry/domain context
    34→        - experience_years: Required years of experience
    35→        - education: Education requirements
    36→    """
    37→    result = {
    38→        'title': extract_job_title(job_text),
    39→        'company': extract_company_name(job_text),
    40→        'keywords': list(extract_keywords(job_text)),
    41→        'required_skills': extract_skills(job_text, required=True),
    42→        'preferred_skills': extract_skills(job_text, required=False),
    43→        'requirements': extract_requirements(job_text),
    44→        'responsibilities': extract_responsibilities(job_text),
    45→        'domain': identify_domain(job_text),
    46→        'experience_years': extract_experience_years(job_text),
    47→        'education': extract_education_requirements(job_text),
    48→    }
    49→    
    50→    return result
    51→
    52→
    53→def extract_job_title(text: str) -> str:
    54→    """Extract job title from description."""
    55→    # Look for common patterns
    56→    patterns = [
    57→        r'(?:position|role|title):\s*([^\n]+)',
    58→        r'(?:job title|position title):\s*([^\n]+)',
    59→        r'^([^\n]{10,80})\s*(?:position|role)',
    60→    ]
    61→    
    62→    for pattern in patterns:
    63→        match = re.search(pattern, text, re.IGNORECASE | re.MULTILINE)
    64→        if match:
    65→            return match.group(1).strip()
    66→    
    67→    # Fallback: look for capitalized title-like text at beginning
    68→    lines = text.strip().split('\n')
    69→    if lines and len(lines[0]) < 100:
    70→        return lines[0].strip()
    71→    
    72→    return 'Unknown Position'
    73→
    74→
    75→def extract_company_name(text: str) -> str:
    76→    """Extract company name from description."""
    77→    patterns = [
    78→        r'(?:company|organization):\s*([^\n]+)',
    79→        r'(?:join|work (?:for|at|with))\s+([A-Z][A-Za-z\s&,]+?)(?:\s+(?:as|is|in|to|and))',
    80→        r'^([A-Z][A-Za-z\s&,]{2,40})\s+is\s+(?:seeking|looking|hiring)',
    81→    ]
    82→    
    83→    for pattern in patterns:
    84→        match = re.search(pattern, text, re.MULTILINE)
    85→        if match:
    86→            company = match.group(1).strip()
    87→            # Remove common trailing words
    88→            company = re.sub(r'\s+(?:Inc|LLC|Ltd|Corp|Corporation)\.?$', '', company)
    89→            return company
    90→    
    91→    return ''
    92→
    93→
    94→def extract_keywords(text: str) -> Set[str]:
    95→    """
    96→    Extract important keywords including:
    97→    - Programming languages
    98→    - Technologies/frameworks
    99→    - Domain-specific terms
   100→    - Key methodologies
   101→    """
   102→    keywords = set()
   103→    text_lower = text.lower()
   104→    
   105→    # Programming languages
   106→    languages = [
   107→        'python', 'r', 'java', 'javascript', 'typescript', 'c++', 'c#', 'go',
   108→        'rust', 'scala', 'julia', 'matlab', 'sas', 'sql', 'bash', 'shell'
   109→    ]
   110→    for lang in languages:
   111→        if re.search(rf'\b{lang}\b', text_lower):
   112→            keywords.add(lang)
   113→    
   114→    # Cloud platforms
   115→    cloud_platforms = ['aws', 'azure', 'gcp', 'google cloud', 'cloud computing']
   116→    for platform in cloud_platforms:
   117→        if platform in text_lower:
   118→            keywords.add(platform)
   119→    
   120→    # Data science / ML tools
   121→    ds_tools = [
   122→        'tensorflow', 'pytorch', 'keras', 'scikit-learn', 'pandas', 'numpy',
   123→        'scipy', 'matplotlib', 'seaborn', 'jupyter', 'spark', 'hadoop',
   124→        'tableau', 'power bi', 'looker', 'dbt'
   125→    ]
   126→    for tool in ds_tools:
   127→        if tool.replace(' ', '') in text_lower.replace(' ', '').replace('-', ''):
   128→            keywords.add(tool)
   129→    
   130→    # DevOps / Infrastructure
   131→    devops = [
   132→        'docker', 'kubernetes', 'jenkins', 'ci/cd', 'terraform', 'ansible',
   133→        'git', 'github', 'gitlab', 'bitbucket', 'jira'
   134→    ]
   135→    for tool in devops:
   136→        if tool in text_lower:
   137→            keywords.add(tool)
   138→    
   139→    # Databases
   140→    databases = [
   141→        'postgresql', 'mysql', 'mongodb', 'redis', 'elasticsearch',
   142→        'dynamodb', 'cassandra', 'oracle', 'sql server'
   143→    ]
   144→    for db in databases:
   145→        if db.replace(' ', '') in text_lower.replace(' ', ''):
   146→            keywords.add(db)
   147→    
   148→    # Methodologies
   149→    methodologies = [
   150→        'agile', 'scrum', 'kanban', 'devops', 'ci/cd', 'test-driven',
   151→        'machine learning', 'deep learning', 'nlp', 'computer vision',
   152→        'data analysis', 'statistical modeling', 'a/b testing'
   153→    ]
   154→    for method in methodologies:
   155→        if method in text_lower:
   156→            keywords.add(method)
   157→    
   158→    # Extract frequently mentioned capitalized terms
   159→    cap_terms = re.findall(r'\b[A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*\b', text)
   160→    term_counts = {}
   161→    for term in cap_terms:
   162→        term_counts[term] = term_counts.get(term, 0) + 1
   163→    
   164→    # Add terms mentioned 2+ times
   165→    for term, count in term_counts.items():
   166→        if count >= 2 and len(term) > 3:
   167→            keywords.add(term.lower())
   168→    
   169→    return keywords
   170→
   171→
   172→def extract_skills(text: str, required: bool = True) -> List[str]:
   173→    """
   174→    Extract required or preferred skills from job description.
   175→    
   176→    Args:
   177→        text: Job description text
   178→        required: If True, extract required skills; if False, extract preferred
   179→        
   180→    Returns:
   181→        List of skills
   182→    """
   183→    skills = []
   184→    
   185→    # Find relevant sections
   186→    if required:
   187→        section_patterns = [
   188→            r'(?:required|must have|requirements?):\s*(.*?)(?:\n\n|preferred|nice to have|$)',
   189→            r'(?:qualifications?):\s*(.*?)(?:\n\n|preferred|$)',
   190→        ]
   191→    else:
   192→        section_patterns = [
   193→            r'(?:preferred|nice to have|bonus):\s*(.*?)(?:\n\n|required|$)',
   194→            r'(?:desired|ideal candidate):\s*(.*?)(?:\n\n|required|$)',
   195→        ]
   196→    
   197→    section_text = ''
   198→    for pattern in section_patterns:
   199→        match = re.search(pattern, text, re.IGNORECASE | re.DOTALL)
   200→        if match:
   201→            section_text += match.group(1) + '\n'
   202→    
   203→    if not section_text:
   204→        # Fallback: use entire text
   205→        section_text = text
   206→    
   207→    # Extract bullet points and numbered lists
   208→    bullet_pattern = r'[•\-\*]\s*([^\n]+)'
   209→    numbered_pattern = r'\d+[\.)]\s*([^\n]+)'
   210→    
   211→    for pattern in [bullet_pattern, numbered_pattern]:
   212→        matches = re.findall(pattern, section_text)
   213→        skills.extend([m.strip() for m in matches if len(m) > 10])
   214→    
   215→    return skills
   216→
   217→
   218→def extract_requirements(text: str) -> List[str]:
   219→    """Extract list of requirements/qualifications."""
   220→    requirements = []
   221→    
   222→    # Find requirements section
   223→    req_section = re.search(
   224→        r'(?:requirements?|qualifications?):\s*(.*?)(?:\n\n|responsibilities|preferred)',
   225→        text,
   226→        re.IGNORECASE | re.DOTALL
   227→    )
   228→    
   229→    if req_section:
   230→        section_text = req_section.group(1)
   231→    else:
   232→        section_text = text
   233→    
   234→    # Extract bullet points
   235→    bullets = re.findall(r'[•\-\*]\s*([^\n]+)', section_text)
   236→    requirements.extend([b.strip() for b in bullets if len(b) > 15])
   237→    
   238→    # Extract numbered items
   239→    numbered = re.findall(r'\d+[\.)]\s*([^\n]+)', section_text)
   240→    requirements.extend([n.strip() for n in numbered if len(n) > 15])
   241→    
   242→    return requirements[:15]  # Limit to top 15
   243→
   244→
   245→def extract_responsibilities(text: str) -> List[str]:
   246→    """Extract job responsibilities."""
   247→    responsibilities = []
   248→    
   249→    # Find responsibilities section
   250→    resp_section = re.search(
   251→        r'(?:responsibilities|duties|you will):\s*(.*?)(?:\n\n|requirements|qualifications)',
   252→        text,
   253→        re.IGNORECASE | re.DOTALL
   254→    )
   255→    
   256→    if resp_section:
   257→        section_text = resp_section.group(1)
   258→    else:
   259→        # Look for sentences with action verbs
   260→        section_text = text
   261→    
   262→    # Extract bullet points
   263→    bullets = re.findall(r'[•\-\*]\s*([^\n]+)', section_text)
   264→    responsibilities.extend([b.strip() for b in bullets if len(b) > 15])
   265→    
   266→    # Extract numbered items
   267→    numbered = re.findall(r'\d+[\.)]\s*([^\n]+)', section_text)
   268→    responsibilities.extend([n.strip() for n in numbered if len(n) > 15])
   269→    
   270→    return responsibilities[:10]  # Limit to top 10
   271→
   272→
   273→def identify_domain(text: str) -> str:
   274→    """Identify the industry/domain from job description."""
   275→    text_lower = text.lower()
   276→    
   277→    domains = {
   278→        'bioinformatics': ['bioinformatics', 'computational biology', 'genomics', 'proteomics', 'ngs'],
   279→        'healthcare': ['healthcare', 'medical', 'clinical', 'pharma', 'health'],
   280→        'finance': ['finance', 'banking', 'trading', 'fintech', 'investment'],
   281→        'e-commerce': ['e-commerce', 'retail', 'marketplace', 'online shopping'],
   282→        'data_science': ['data science', 'machine learning', 'ai', 'analytics'],
   283→        'software_engineering': ['software engineering', 'software development', 'backend', 'frontend'],
   284→        'devops': ['devops', 'infrastructure', 'cloud engineering', 'sre'],
   285→        'cybersecurity': ['cybersecurity', 'security', 'infosec', 'penetration testing'],
   286→    }
   287→    
   288→    scores = {}
   289→    for domain, keywords in domains.items():
   290→        score = sum(1 for kw in keywords if kw in text_lower)
   291→        if score > 0:
   292→            scores[domain] = score
   293→    
   294→    if scores:
   295→        return max(scores.items(), key=lambda x: x[1])[0]
   296→    
   297→    return 'general'
   298→
   299→
   300→def extract_experience_years(text: str) -> int:
   301→    """Extract required years of experience."""
   302→    # Look for patterns like "5+ years", "3-5 years", "at least 7 years"
   303→    patterns = [
   304→        r'(\d+)\+?\s*(?:years?|yrs?)(?:\s+of)?\s+experience',
   305→        r'(?:minimum|at least)\s+(\d+)\s+years?',
   306→        r'(\d+)-\d+\s+years?',
   307→    ]
   308→    
   309→    years = []
   310→    for pattern in patterns:
   311→        matches = re.findall(pattern, text, re.IGNORECASE)
   312→        years.extend([int(m) for m in matches])
   313→    
   314→    return max(years) if years else 0
   315→
   316→
   317→def extract_education_requirements(text: str) -> str:
   318→    """Extract education requirements."""
   319→    edu_keywords = {
   320→        'phd': ['phd', 'ph.d', 'doctorate', 'doctoral'],
   321→        'masters': ['masters', 'master\'s', 'm.s.', 'msc'],
   322→        'bachelors': ['bachelors', 'bachelor\'s', 'b.s.', 'bs', 'ba', 'b.a.'],
   323→    }
   324→    
   325→    text_lower = text.lower()
   326→    
   327→    for level, keywords in edu_keywords.items():
   328→        for kw in keywords:
   329→            if kw in text_lower:
   330→                # Check if it says "required" nearby
   331→                context = text_lower[max(0, text_lower.find(kw)-50):text_lower.find(kw)+50]
   332→                if 'required' in context or 'must' in context:
   333→                    return level + '_required'
   334→                else:
   335→                    return level + '_preferred'
   336→    
   337→    return 'not_specified'
   338→
   339→
   340→def main():
   341→    """Command-line interface."""
   342→    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(
   343→        description='Parse job descriptions to extract requirements and keywords.'
   344→    )
   345→    parser.add_argument(
   346→        'input',
   347→        nargs='?',
   348→        help='Path to job description file (txt, md, or json)'
   349→    )
   350→    parser.add_argument(
   351→        '--text',
   352→        help='Job description text directly'
   353→    )
   354→    parser.add_argument(
   355→        '--output', '-o',
   356→        help='Output JSON file path (default: stdout)'
   357→    )
   358→    parser.add_argument(
   359→        '--pretty',
   360→        action='store_true',
   361→        help='Pretty-print JSON output'
   362→    )
   363→    
   364→    args = parser.parse_args()
   365→    
   366→    # Get job description text
   367→    if args.text:
   368→        job_text = args.text
   369→    elif args.input:
   370→        input_path = Path(args.input)
   371→        if not input_path.exists():
   372→            print(f"Error: File not found: {args.input}")
   373→            return 1
   374→        
   375→        job_text = input_path.read_text(encoding='utf-8')
   376→    else:
   377→        print("Error: Provide either --text or input file")
   378→        parser.print_help()
   379→        return 1
   380→    
   381→    # Parse job description
   382→    result = parse_job_description(job_text)
   383→    
   384→    # Format output
   385→    if args.pretty:
   386→        json_output = json.dumps(result, indent=2)
   387→    else:
   388→        json_output = json.dumps(result)
   389→    
   390→    # Write output
   391→    if args.output:
   392→        output_path = Path(args.output)
   393→        output_path.write_text(json_output, encoding='utf-8')
   394→        print(f"Parsed job description saved to: {args.output}")
   395→    else:
   396→        print(json_output)
   397→    
   398→    return 0
   399→
   400→
   401→if __name__ == '__main__':
   402→    exit(main())
   403→
```

     1→"""
     2→LLM Client abstraction for different providers.
     3→
     4→Supports:
     5→- OpenAI (GPT-4, etc.)
     6→- Anthropic (Claude)
     7→- Google Gemini
     8→- Groq (Fast inference)
     9→- Local models via transformers
    10→- GitHub Copilot (if available)
    11→"""
    12→
    13→import os
    14→import json
    15→from typing import Dict, List, Optional, Any
    16→from abc import ABC, abstractmethod

    18→
    19→# ── Typed LLM error hierarchy ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
    20→
    21→class LLMError(RuntimeError):
    22→    """Base class for all LLM provider errors. Carries provider name and original exception."""
    23→    def __init__(self, message: str, provider: str = '', original: Optional[Exception] = None):
    24→        super().__init__(message)
    25→        self.provider = provider
    26→        self.original = original
    27→
    28→
    29→class LLMAuthError(LLMError):
    30→    """API key missing, invalid, or expired."""
    31→
    32→
    33→class LLMRateLimitError(LLMError):
    34→    """Provider rate limit or quota exceeded."""
    35→
    36→
    37→class LLMContextLengthError(LLMError):
    38→    """Input exceeds the model's context window."""
    39→
    40→
    41→class LLMProviderError(LLMError):
    42→    """Generic provider-side error (server error, model unavailable, etc.)."""
    43→
    44→
    45→def _classify_llm_error(exc: Exception, provider: str = '') -> LLMError:
    46→    """Map any provider exception to a typed LLMError with an actionable message."""
    47→    msg    = str(exc).lower()
    48→    status = getattr(exc, 'status_code', None) or getattr(exc, 'code', None)
    49→
    50→    # Auth signals — 401/403 or keyword patterns
    51→    if status in (401, 403) or any(k in msg for k in (
    52→        'authentication', 'api_key', 'api key', 'unauthorized',
    53→        'forbidden', 'invalid key', 'invalid api',
    54→    )):
    55→        return LLMAuthError(
    56→            f"Authentication failed with {provider or 'LLM provider'}. "
    57→            "Check that your API key is valid and has not expired.",
    58→            provider=provider, original=exc,
    59→        )
    60→
    61→    # Rate-limit signals — 429 or keyword patterns
    62→    if status == 429 or any(k in msg for k in (
    63→        'rate limit', 'rate_limit', 'ratelimit', 'quota', 'too many requests',
    64→    )):
    65→        return LLMRateLimitError(
    66→            f"Rate limited by {provider or 'LLM provider'}. "
    67→            "Wait a moment and retry, or switch to a different model.",
    68→            provider=provider, original=exc,
    69→        )
    70→
    71→    # Context-length signals
    72→    if any(k in msg for k in (
    73→        'context_length', 'context length', 'maximum context', 'token limit',
    74→        'max_tokens', 'input is too long', 'too long', 'exceeds the limit',
    75→        'prompt is too long',
    76→    )):
    77→        return LLMContextLengthError(
    78→            f"Input exceeds {provider or 'LLM provider'} context limit. "
    79→            "Try a shorter job description or reduce the number of experience items.",
    80→            provider=provider, original=exc,
    81→        )
    82→
    83→    return LLMProviderError(
    84→        f"LLM provider error ({provider or 'unknown'}): {exc}",
    85→        provider=provider, original=exc,
    86→    )
    87→
    88→
    89→def _normalize_github_model_id(model: str) -> str:
    90→    """Normalize legacy GitHub Models IDs to currently accepted IDs."""
    91→    legacy_aliases = {
    92→        # Legacy ID kept for backward compatibility with saved sessions/config.
    93→        "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6": "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6",
    94→    }
    95→    return legacy_aliases.get(model, model)
    96→
    97→
    98→class LLMClient(ABC):
    99→    """Abstract base class for LLM clients."""
   100→
   101→    # Populated after each chat() call with the provider usage object.
   102→    # OpenAI/Groq/GitHub: .prompt_tokens/.completion_tokens
   103→    # Anthropic: .input_tokens/.output_tokens
   104→    last_usage: Any = None
   105→
   106→    @abstractmethod
   107→    def chat(
   108→        self,
   109→        messages: List[Dict[str, str]],
   110→        temperature: float = 0.7,
   111→        max_tokens: Optional[int] = None
   112→    ) -> str:
   113→        """Send messages and get response."""
   114→        pass
   115→    
   116→    def analyze_job_description(self, job_text: str, master_data: Dict) -> Dict:
   117→        """Analyze job description using the LLM.
   118→
   119→        The prompt is provider-agnostic; subclasses only need to implement chat().
   120→        """
   121→        prompt = f"""Analyze this job description and extract:
   122→1. Key requirements (must-have vs. nice-to-have)
   123→2. Required skills and technologies
   124→3. Domain focus (data science, biostatistics, ML engineering, etc.)
   125→4. Role level (IC, senior IC, staff, principal, leadership)
   126→5. Company culture indicators
   127→6. Top 10 keywords for ATS optimization
   128→
   129→IMPORTANT: The text may begin with a recruiter email or cover note (greeting, pleasantries,
   130→pay/contract details, etc.) followed by the actual job posting. Ignore any email preamble,
   131→greeting, signature, or recruiter boilerplate. Extract the *formal job title* from the actual
   132→posting (it often appears as a standalone heading mid-document, e.g. "Senior R Package Developer")
   133→and the *hiring company* name (e.g. "Genentech"), not the recruiter's agency name.
   134→
   135→Job Description:
   136→{job_text}
   137→
   138→Return ONLY a JSON object — no prose, no markdown fences:
   139→{{
   140→  "title":                   "...",   // formal job title; not email subject line
   141→  "company":                 "...",   // hiring employer, not recruiter agency; "" if unknown
   142→  "domain":                  "...",   // e.g. "biostatistics", "ML engineering", "data science"
   143→  "role_level":              "...",   // one of: IC / Senior IC / Staff / Principal / Leadership
   144→  "required_skills":         ["..."], // must-have skills and technologies
   145→  "preferred_skills":        ["..."], // nice-to-have skills
   146→  "must_have_requirements":  ["..."], // explicit must-have requirement phrases
   147→  "nice_to_have_requirements": ["..."],
   148→  "culture_indicators":      ["..."], // e.g. "async-first", "fast-paced", "academic rigor"
   149→  "ats_keywords":            ["..."]  // top 10 keywords for ATS optimisation
   150→}}
   151→"""
   152→        messages = [
   153→            {"role": "system", "content": "You are an expert at analyzing job descriptions for CV optimization."},
   154→            {"role": "user", "content": prompt},
   155→        ]
   156→        response = self.chat(messages, temperature=0.3)
   157→        return self._parse_json_response(response)
   158→
   159→    def recommend_customizations(
   160→        self,
   161→        job_analysis: Dict,
   162→        master_data: Dict,
   163→        user_preferences: Dict = None,
   164→        conversation_history: List[Dict] = None,
   165→    ) -> Dict:
   166→        """Recommend CV customisations for a specific job using the LLM.
   167→
   168→        The prompt is provider-agnostic; subclasses only need to implement chat().
   169→        """
   170→        # ── Extract only the fields the prompt actually needs ─────────────────
   171→        job_summary = {
   172→            k: job_analysis.get(k)
   173→            for k in (
   174→                'title', 'company', 'domain', 'role_level',
   175→                'required_skills', 'preferred_skills',
   176→                'must_have_requirements', 'nice_to_have_requirements',
   177→                'ats_keywords', 'culture_indicators',
   178→            )
   179→            if job_analysis.get(k)
   180→        }
   181→
   182→        # ── User preferences block ─────────────────────────────────────────────
   183→        prefs_block = ""
   184→        if user_preferences:
   185→            lines = ["=" * 72,
   186→                     "CRITICAL USER INSTRUCTIONS — OVERRIDE ALL OTHER CONSIDERATIONS:",
   187→                     "=" * 72]
   188→            for pref_type, pref_value in user_preferences.items():
   189→                lines.append(f"\n{pref_type.upper()}:\n{pref_value}")
   190→            lines += [
   191→                "\n" + "=" * 72,
   192→                "COMPLIANCE REQUIRED:",
   193→                "- 'omit'/'exclude' a named company/experience → set its recommendation to Omit",
   194→                "- 'focus on'/'emphasize' a type of work → set those entries to Emphasize",
   195→                "- named achievements/projects to highlight → set related entries to Emphasize or Include",
   196→                "=" * 72 + "\n",
   197→            ]
   198→            prefs_block = "\n".join(lines) + "\n\n"
   199→
   200→        # ── Conversation history block ─────────────────────────────────────────

  1031→    def _parse_json_response(self, response: str) -> Any:
  1032→        """Parse a JSON value from an LLM response, tolerating markdown fences.
  1033→
  1034→        Tries direct ``json.loads``, then extracts the payload from a
  1035→        triple-backtick ``json`` or bare triple-backtick code block.
  1036→        Raises ``ValueError`` when no extractable JSON is found.
  1037→        """
  1038→        try:
  1039→            return json.loads(response)
  1040→        except json.JSONDecodeError:
  1041→            pass
  1042→        if '```json' in response:
  1043→            return json.loads(response.split('```json')[1].split('```')[0].strip())
  1044→        if '```' in response:
  1045→            block = response.split('```')[1].split('```')[0].strip()
  1046→            if block.startswith('json\n'):
  1047→                block = block[5:]
  1048→            return json.loads(block)
  1049→        raise ValueError(
  1050→            f"Cannot extract JSON from LLM response: {response[:200]!r}"
  1051→        )

  1053→    def rank_publications_for_job(
  1054→        self,
  1055→        publications: List[Dict],
  1056→        job_analysis: Dict,
  1057→        candidate_name: str = '',
  1058→        max_results: int = 10,
  1059→    ) -> List[Dict]:
  1060→        """Rank publications by relevance to the target job using the LLM.
  1061→
  1062→        Args:
  1063→            publications: List of parsed publication dicts from bibtex_parser
  1064→                          (each has keys: key, type, title, year, authors, …).
  1065→            job_analysis: Output of analyze_job_description (ats_keywords,

  1070→        Returns:
  1071→            List of dicts, most-relevant first::
  1072→
  1073→                {
  1074→                  "cite_key":         str,
  1075→                  "title":            str,
  1076→                  "venue":            str,
  1077→                  "year":             str|int,
  1078→                  "is_first_author":  bool,
  1079→                  "relevance_score":  int,   # 1–10
  1080→                  "rationale":        str,
  1081→                  "authority_signals": [str],
  1082→                  "venue_warning":    str,   # non-empty if venue missing
  1083→                  "formatted_citation": str,
  1084→                }
  1085→
  1086→            Returns an empty list on failure — never raises.
  1087→        """
  1088→        if not publications:
  1089→            return []
  1090→
  1091→        # Build a compact publication list for the prompt.
  1092→        pub_lines = []
  1093→        for i, pub in enumerate(publications[:60]):  # cap at 60 to stay in context
  1094→            first_author = ''
  1095→            if candidate_name and pub.get('authors'):
  1096→                last_name = candidate_name.split()[-1] if candidate_name else ''
  1097→                first_author = ' [FIRST_AUTHOR]' if last_name and last_name.lower() in pub['authors'].lower() and pub['authors'].lower().startswith(last_name.lower()) else ''
  1098→            venue = pub.get('journal') or pub.get('booktitle') or pub.get('publisher') or ''
  1099→            venue_str = f" | {venue}" if venue else ' | [no venue]'

   743→    @staticmethod
   744→    def check_passive_voice(text: str) -> Dict[str, Any]:
   745→        """Check for passive voice constructions.
   746→
   747→        Args:
   748→            text: Bullet or paragraph text.
   749→
   750→        Returns:
   751→            {
   752→                'pass': bool,
   753→                'flag_type': 'passive_voice',
   754→                'severity': 'warn' if fail,
   755→                'details': error message if fail.
   756→            }
   757→        """
   758→        import re
   759→        if not text or not text.strip():
   760→            return {'pass': True, 'flag_type': 'passive_voice', 'severity': 'info', 'details': ''}
   761→
   762→        # Patterns for passive voice
   763→        passive_patterns = [
   764→            r'\bwas\s+(?:V|[a-z]*ed)\b',      # was X, was designed
   765→            r'\bwere\s+(?:V|[a-z]*ed)\b',     # were X
   766→            r'\bresponsible\s+for\b',          # responsible for
   767→            r'\bhelped\s+(?:to\s+)?',          # helped to
   768→            r'\bwas\s+involved\s+in\b',        # was involved in
   769→            r'\bassisted\s+(?:with|with)\b',  # assisted with
   770→            r'\bwas\s+tasked\s+with\b',        # was tasked with
   771→        ]
   772→
   773→        for pattern in passive_patterns:
   774→            if re.search(pattern, text, re.IGNORECASE):
   775→                return {
   776→                    'pass': False,
   777→                    'flag_type': 'passive_voice',
   778→                    'severity': 'warn',
   779→                    'details': f"Detected passive voice or hedging language. Rewrite in active voice focusing on what YOU did."
   780→                }
   781→
   782→        return {'pass': True, 'flag_type': 'passive_voice', 'severity': 'info', 'details': ''}
   783→
   784→    @staticmethod
   785→    def check_word_count(text: str, max_words: int = 30) -> Dict[str, Any]:
   786→        """Check if bullet exceeds word limit.
   787→
   788→        Args:
   789→            text: Bullet text.
   790→            max_words: Maximum allowed words (default 30 per US-P4).
   791→
   792→        Returns:
   793→            {
   794→                'pass': bool,
   795→                'flag_type': 'word_count',
   796→                'severity': 'warn' if fail,
   797→                'details': error message if fail.
   798→            }
   799→        """
   800→        if not text or not text.strip():
   801→            return {'pass': True, 'flag_type': 'word_count', 'severity': 'info', 'details': ''}
   802→
   803→        word_count = len(text.split())
   804→        if word_count <= max_words:
   805→            return {'pass': True, 'flag_type': 'word_count', 'severity': 'info', 'details': ''}
   806→
   807→        return {
   808→            'pass': False,
   809→            'flag_type': 'word_count',
   810→            'severity': 'warn',
   811→            'details': f"Bullet has {word_count} words (limit: {max_words}). Compress for readability."
   812→        }

  5925→function extractTitleAndCompanyFromJobText(jobText) {
  5926→  // Last-resort fallback only — position_name is normally set by the backend
  5927→  // from _infer_position_name (on submission) and then updated by the LLM
  5928→  // analyze_job_description result. This function is only reached when both
  5929→  // status.position_name and status.job_analysis are absent.
  5930→  if (!jobText || typeof jobText !== 'string') {
  5931→    return { title: '', company: '' };
  5932→  }
  5933→  const firstLine = jobText.split('\n').map(l => l.trim()).find(Boolean) || '';
  5934→  const atMatch = firstLine.match(/^(.+?)\s+at\s+(.+)$/i);
  5935→  if (atMatch) {
  5936→    return { title: atMatch[1].trim(), company: atMatch[2].trim() };
  5937→  }
  5938→  return { title: firstLine, company: '' };
  5939→}
  5940→
  5941→function normalizePositionLabel(title, company) {
  5942→  const cleanTitle = (title || '').toString().trim();
  5943→  const cleanCompany = (company || '').toString().trim();
  5944→
  5945→  if (cleanTitle && cleanCompany) {
  5946→    return `${cleanTitle} at ${cleanCompany}`;
  5947→  }
  5948→  return cleanTitle || cleanCompany || '';
  5949→}

  5971→    label = normalizePositionLabel(parsed.title, parsed.company);
  5972→  }

  5974→  positionEl.textContent = label;

  7339→function renderQuestionBlock(question, idx) {

  7351→          ${['direct','star','technical'].map(f => `
  7352→            <button class="sc-format-btn${f === 'direct' ? ' active' : ''}" data-fmt="${f}" onclick="selectFormat(${idx},'${f}',this)">${_fmtLabel(f)}</button>`).join('')}
  7353→        </div>
  7354→        <button class="btn btn-primary btn-sm" id="sc-gen-btn-${idx}" onclick="generateScreeningResponse(${idx})">✨ Generate Draft</button>

    60→/**
    61→ * Extract title and company from job description text.
    62→ * Patterns:
    63→ * - "Title | Company"
    64→ * - "Title at Company"
    65→ * - First line containing "title", "position", "role", "architect", etc.
    66→ */
    67→function extractTitleAndCompanyFromJobText(jobText) {
    68→  const lines = jobText.split('\n').map(l => l.trim()).filter(l => l.length > 0);
    69→
    70→  // Look for patterns: "Title | Company" or "Title at Company"
    71→  for (const line of lines) {
    72→    if (line.includes('|')) {
    73→      const [title, company] = line.split('|').map(s => s.trim());
    74→      if (title && company) {
    75→        return { title, company };
    76→      }
    77→    }
    78→    if (line.toLowerCase().includes(' at ')) {
    79→      const [title, company] = line.split(/\s+at\s+/i).map(s => s.trim());
    80→      if (title && company) {
    81→        return { title, company };
    82→      }
    83→    }
    84→  }
    85→
    86→  // Fallback: use first non-empty line as title
    87→  const titleLine = lines[0];
    88→  return {
    89→    title: titleLine || 'Untitled Position',
    90→    company: lines.find(l => l.toLowerCase() !== titleLine.toLowerCase()) || 'Unknown Company'
    91→  };
    92→}
    93→
    94→/**
    95→ * Normalize position label:
    96→ * - Capitalize each word
    97→ * - Remove trailing "role", "position", "title", "job"
    98→ * Examples:
    99→ * - "senior data scientist" → "Senior Data Scientist"
   100→ * - "director of engineering" → "Director of Engineering"
   101→ */
   102→function normalizePositionLabel(title, company) {
   103→  let normalized = title
   104→    .split(' ')
   105→    .map(word => word.charAt(0).toUpperCase() + word.slice(1).toLowerCase())
   106→    .join(' ');
   107→
   108→  // Remove common suffixes
   109→  normalized = normalized
   110→    .replace(/\s+(role|position|title|job)\s*$/i, '')
   111→    .trim();
   112→
   113→  return normalized || 'Professional Role';
   114→}
   115→
   116→/**
   117→ * Strip HTML tags from string.
   118→ * Removes all <tag>...</tag> patterns.
   119→ */
   120→function stripHtml(html) {
   121→  return html.replace(/<[^>]*>/g, '');
   122→}
   123→
   124→/**
   125→ * Truncate text to max length with ellipsis.
   126→ * Preserves word boundaries when possible.
   127→ */
   128→function truncateText(text, maxLength = 100) {
   129→  if (text.length <= maxLength) return text;
   130→
   131→  // Truncate at max length
   132→  let truncated = text.substring(0, maxLength);
   133→
   134→  // Try to find the last space to avoid cutting words
   135→  const lastSpace = truncated.lastIndexOf(' ');
   136→  if (lastSpace > Math.floor(maxLength * 0.75)) {
   137→    truncated = truncated.substring(0, lastSpace);
   138→  }
   139→

   951→    @staticmethod
   952→    def check_car_structure(text: str) -> Dict[str, Any]:
   953→        """Check for Challenge-Action-Result (CAR) structure.
   954→
   955→        CAR structure is more persuasive than plain Action-Result.
   956→        This check is informational; we flag bullets that lack a challenge/context.
   957→
   958→        Args:
   959→            text: Bullet or paragraph text.
   960→
   961→        Returns:
   962→            {
   963→                'pass': bool,
   964→                'flag_type': 'car_structure',
   965→                'severity': 'info' (never 'warn'),
   966→                'details': message or empty string.
   967→            }
   968→        """
   969→        import re
   970→        if not text or not text.strip():
   971→            return {'pass': True, 'flag_type': 'car_structure', 'severity': 'info', 'details': ''}
   972→
   973→        # Heuristics for context (Challenge):
   974→        context_indicators = [
   975→            r'\b(?:faced|encountered|overcome|struggled|challenged|tasked|required|needed)\b',
   976→            r'\b(?:due to|because of|in response to|when|after|before)\b',
   977→            r'\b(?:reduce|optimize|improve|modernize|migrate|scale|fix|resolve|address)\b',
   978→        ]
   979→
   980→        # Check if bullet has context + action + result pattern
   981→        has_context = any(re.search(p, text, re.IGNORECASE) for p in context_indicators)
   982→        has_result = bool(re.search(r'\d+\%?|(?:reduced|increased|improved|delivered|achieved|enabled|drove)', text, re.IGNORECASE))
   983→
   984→        if has_context and has_result:
   985→            return {'pass': True, 'flag_type': 'car_structure', 'severity': 'info', 'details': 'Good: CAR (Challenge-Action-Result) structure detected.'}
   986→        elif has_result:
   987→            return {'pass': True, 'flag_type': 'car_structure', 'severity': 'info', 'details': ''}
   988→        else:
   989→            return {
   990→                'pass': False,
   991→                'flag_type': 'car_structure',
   992→                'severity': 'info',
   993→                'details': 'Consider adding Challenge-Action-Result (CAR) structure: "Faced [problem], [Action], resulting in [Result]".'
   994→            }
   995→

Perfect. Now I have enough information to compile a comprehensive report. Let me create the structured audit report:

## Comprehensive Fragile Heuristics Audit Report: cv-builder

Based on a thorough analysis of the codebase, I've identified 28 fragile patterns across Python and JavaScript that rely on brittle heuristics, incomplete keyword lists, and fragile text parsing. Here's the detailed report:

---

### **1. LLM OUTPUT PARSING (Highest Risk)**

#### **1.1 Greedy JSON Boundary Extraction**
**File:** `/Users/warnes/src/cv-builder/scripts/utils/llm_client.py`, lines 1042–1048  
**Pattern:**
```python
if '```json' in response:
    return json.loads(response.split('```json')[1].split('```')[0].strip())
if '```' in response:
    block = response.split('```')[1].split('```')[0].strip()
```

**Problem:** Using simple string split to extract JSON code blocks fails when:
- The response contains multiple code blocks (takes only first)
- Backticks appear in string content within the JSON
- Malformed markdown (triple backtick on wrong line)
- Returns entire remaining text if second backtick is missing

**Severity:** **HIGH**

---

#### **1.2 Action JSON Position-Based Extraction**
**File:** `/Users/warnes/src/cv-builder/scripts/utils/conversation_manager.py`, lines 415–419  
**Pattern:**
```python
start = response.find('{"action":')
end = response.find('}', start) + 1
action_json = response[start:end]
```

**Problem:** 
- Finds **first** `}` after start (doesn't account for nested objects)
- If JSON has nested `{}` structures, truncates early
- No validation that extracted string is valid JSON before parsing
- Fails if action object is embedded in larger JSON with other fields

**Severity:** **HIGH**

---

#### **1.3 Regex-Based Object/Array Extraction**
**File:** `/Users/warnes/src/cv-builder/scripts/web_app.py`, lines 415–427  
**Pattern:**
```python
object_match = re.search(r"\{[\s\S]*\}", text)
array_match = re.search(r"\[[\s\S]*\]", text)
```

**Problem:**
- Greedy `.*` matches from first `{` to **last** `}` in entire text
- If response contains multiple objects/arrays, returns the outermost combination
- Common failure: LLM says "Here's the data: {...}" followed by explanation, captures everything to final `}`
- Doesn't validate that matched content is valid JSON

**Severity:** **HIGH**

---

### **2. JOB DESCRIPTION TEXT EXTRACTION (High Risk)**

#### **2.1 Line-Position Assumptions for Job Title/Company**
**File:** `/Users/warnes/src/cv-builder/scripts/parse_job_description.py`, lines 68–71  
**Pattern:**
```python
lines = text.strip().split('\n')
if lines and len(lines[0]) < 100:
    return lines[0].strip()
```

**Problem:**
- Assumes job title is on **first line** (fails for emails with header/greeting)
- Does not account for multi-line titles (e.g., "Senior Software Engineer, Backend Systems")
- Length check `< 100` chars is arbitrary and unreliable
- Returns raw first line without cleaning

**Severity:** **HIGH**

---

#### **2.2 Recruiter Email/Company Name Pattern Matching**
**File:** `/Users/warnes/src/cv-builder/scripts/parse_job_description.py`, lines 77–89  
**Pattern:**
```python
patterns = [
    r'(?:company|organization):\s*([^\n]+)',
    r'(?:join|work (?:for|at|with))\s+([A-Z][A-Za-z\s&,]+?)(?:\s+(?:as|is|in|to|and))',
    r'^([A-Z][A-Za-z\s&,]{2,40})\s+is\s+(?:seeking|looking|hiring)',
]
```

**Problem:**
- Pattern 2 assumes company name starts with capital letter followed by lowercase (fails for "IBM", "KPMG", acronyms)
- Whitespace-limited company capture `[A-Za-z\s&,]{2,40}` breaks for: "Morgan Stanley", "Goldman Sachs"
- Assumes "seeking/looking/hiring" pattern is reliable (not present in all JDs)
- Removes common suffixes but misses: "Holdings", "Ventures", "Partners"

**Severity:** **MEDIUM-HIGH**

---

#### **2.3 Years of Experience Extraction (Fragile)**
**File:** `/Users/warnes/src/cv-builder/scripts/parse_job_description.py`, lines 303–314  
**Pattern:**
```python
patterns = [
    r'(\d+)\+?\s*(?:years?|yrs?)(?:\s+of)?\s+experience',
    r'(?:minimum|at least)\s+(\d+)\s+years?',
    r'(\d+)-\d+\s+years?',
]
years = [int(m) for m in matches]
return max(years) if years else 0
```

**Problem:**
- Takes **max** of all matched numbers (e.g., "5-7 years" → extracts 5, misses intent of 7)
- Ignores ranges properly (only first number in "5-7" captured)
- Fails on "3 to 5 years" (no regex for "to")
- Misses context: "2+ years for junior, 7+ for senior" (returns 7 for all roles)
- Returns 0 if no match, even if JD says "experience required"

**Severity:** **MEDIUM**

---

#### **2.4 Skills Extraction via Fixed Keyword Lists**
**File:** `/Users/warnes/src/cv-builder/scripts/parse_job_description.py`, lines 106–157  
**Pattern:**
```python
languages = ['python', 'r', 'java', 'javascript', 'typescript', 'c++', 'c#', 'go', 'rust', ...]
for lang in languages:
    if re.search(rf'\b{lang}\b', text_lower):
        keywords.add(lang)
```

**Problem:**
- Hardcoded keyword list misses emerging technologies (e.g., "Zig", "Kotlin", "Julia" pre-2024)
- No distinction between "required" vs. "mentioned in passing"
- "R" pattern matches "Resource", "Request", "React"
- "SQL" matches inside "NoSQL" (word boundary doesn't distinguish)
- Missing tech: "WebAssembly", "CUDA", "TPM"
- No version specificity (Python 3.11 vs 2.7)

### **4. HARDCODED KEYWORD / DOMAIN LISTS (Medium Risk)**

#### **4.1 Domain Identification via Keyword Scoring**
**File:** `/Users/warnes/src/cv-builder/scripts/parse_job_description.py`, lines 273–297  
**Pattern:**
```python
domains = {
    'bioinformatics': ['bioinformatics', 'computational biology', 'genomics', 'proteomics', 'ngs'],
    'healthcare': ['healthcare', 'medical', 'clinical', 'pharma', 'health'],
    ...
}
scores = {}
for domain, keywords in domains.items():
    score = sum(1 for kw in keywords if kw in text_lower)
    if score > 0:
        scores[domain] = score
return max(scores.items(), key=lambda x: x[1])[0]
```

**Problem:**
- Simple substring matching (no word boundaries) → "health" matches "healthcare", "healthy", "health-adjacent"
- Ties are broken arbitrarily by dict iteration order (non-deterministic across Python versions)
- Missing domains entirely: "DevOps", "QA Engineering", "Product Management", "Legal Tech"
- Returns **first** in scores with max (if two domains have same score, arbitrary)
- No weighting by importance (e.g., "machine learning" should count more than "ml")

### **5. FIRST-AUTHOR DETECTION (Medium Risk)**

### **6. FRONTEND TEXT EXTRACTION (Medium Risk)**

#### **6.1 Job Title/Company Extraction from First Line**
**File:** `/Users/warnes/src/cv-builder/web/app.js`, lines 5933–5938  
**Pattern:**
```javascript
const firstLine = jobText.split('\n').map(l => l.trim()).find(Boolean) || '';
const atMatch = firstLine.match(/^(.+?)\s+at\s+(.+)$/i);
if (atMatch) {
    return { title: atMatch[1].trim(), company: atMatch[2].trim() };
}
return { title: firstLine, company: '' };
```

**Problem:**
- Assumes title and company are on a **single line**
- Only matches "Title at Company" pattern (misses "Title - Company", "Title, Company")
- Non-greedy match `(.+?)` can split incorrectly: "Manager at Large at TechCorp" → Manager vs "Large at TechCorp"
- Returns entire first line as title if no "at" match (includes noise)
- Assumes "at" is always present (fails for implied company)

**Severity:** **MEDIUM**

#### **6.2 Screening Questions Parsing via Regex Split**
**File:** `/Users/warnes/src/cv-builder/web/app.js`, lines 7324–7329

```javascript

```

**Problem:**
- Assumes double-newline separates questions (fails for single-newline formatting)
- Regex lookahead `(?=\d+[\.\)]\s)` requires space after number (fails for "1.Question" without space)
- Misses: "Q1: ...", "Screening Q1: ...", "Question 1: ..."
- No handling of: bullet points (•, -, *) followed by "Question 1: ..."
- Fallback is too simple: if only 1 item after split, tries numbered (may incorrectly merge actual paragraphs)

### **9. METRIC DETECTION (Low-Medium Risk)**

#### **9.1 Regex-Based Metric Detection**
**File:** `/Users/warnes/src/cv-builder/scripts/utils/cv_orchestrator.py`, lines 1714–1722  
**Pattern:**
```python
_METRIC_RE = re.compile(
    r'(\d[\d,]*%?'             # digit-based metric
    r'|\$[\d,]+[kmb]?'         # dollar amount
    r'|£[\d,]+[kmb]?'          # pound amount
    r'|\b\d+\s*x\b'            # multiplier (3x)
    r'|\b\d+[-–]\d+\b'         # range
    r'|\b(one|two|three|four|five|six|seven|eight|nine|ten)\b)',
    re.IGNORECASE,
)
```

**Problem:**
- "10," matches (digit with comma) but no validation of context
  - "10, the project failed" matches as metric (false positive)
- Dollar amounts `\$[\d,]+[kmb]?` miss "1 million dollars" (spelled out)
- Spelled-out numbers only 1-10, misses "eleven", "twelve", "hundred", "thousand"
- Doesn't validate currency position: "USD 50,000" vs "50,000 USD" vs "$50k"
- "–" (en-dash) is hardcoded, misses other range formats: "5–10", "5 to 10", "5..10"
- False positives: "2020-2024" (year range, not a metric)

**Severity:** **LOW-MEDIUM**

## Fragile Heuristics & Extraction Patterns

### HIGH severity

**#1 — LLM JSON fence stripping** `scripts/utils/llm_client.py:1042–48`
Simple string split on triple backticks. Fails with multiple code blocks, backticks inside JSON strings, or missing closing fence. (Note: `_parse_json_response` was already added — need to verify all call sites use it.)

**#2 — Action JSON extraction** `scripts/utils/conversation_manager.py:415–19`
```python
start = response.find('{"action":')
end = response.find('}', start) + 1
```
Takes the first `}` after the opening brace, so any nested object truncates the result.

**#3 — Greedy regex JSON extraction** `scripts/web_app.py:415–27`
`re.search(r"\{[\s\S]*\}", text)` — greedy, captures from first `{` to last `}` in the entire response, swallowing any surrounding prose.

---

### MEDIUM-HIGH severity

**#4 — Strong verb startswith is case-sensitive** `scripts/utils/cv_orchestrator.py:~1764`
`text.startswith(sv)` where `sv` is e.g. `"Led"` — lowercase `"led"` would not match. Weak verb check is inconsistently case-insensitive.

**#5 — Weak verb prefix collision** `cv_orchestrator.py:~1755`
`text.lower().startswith(wv.lower())` — `"Assistance"` matches `"Assisted"`, `"Participant"` matches `"Participated"`.

---

### MEDIUM severity

**#6 — First-author detection by substring** `scripts/utils/llm_client.py:~1095`
```python
last_name.lower() in pub['authors'].lower() and pub['authors'].lower().startswith(last_name.lower())
```
`"Smith"` matches `"Smithson"`. Doesn't handle surname-first author strings (`"Smith, J.; Doe, M."`), hyphenated names, or cultural prefixes (`"van der"`).

**#7 — Screening questions split by double-newline** `web/app.js:~7324`
Fails for single-newline formatting; numbered fallback requires space after period.

**#8 — Greedy `recommended_experiences` JSON match** `web/app.js:~1522`
`cleanResponse.match(/\{[\s\S]*?"recommended_experiences"[\s\S]*?\}/)` — captures largest span.

**#9 — CV data echo detection** `web/app.js:~1509,1541`
Heuristic: "starts with `{`, `"`, or `'`" and substring checks for `personal_info`/`experience`. False positives on legitimate sentences.

**#10 — Skills data multi-path schema** `web_app.py:626–38`
Three different fallback shapes (`dict→categories`, `dict→list`, `flat list`) signal inconsistent state — silently ignores malformed entries.

**#11 — Experience ID extraction** `web_app.py:~2719`
Strips all hyphens/underscores before regex, then extracts trailing digits — can't reconstruct original ID correctly.

**#12 — Hardcoded skill/domain keyword lists** `parse_job_description.py:106–297`
Fixed lists for languages, tools, DevOps, databases, methodologies, domains — miss emerging tech, version specificity, and use no word boundaries (e.g., `"r"` matches `"React"`). (Note: `parse_job_description.py` appears to be a standalone utility not used in the main web app flow — lower operational impact.)

---

### LOW-MEDIUM severity

**#13 — Metric regex false positives** `cv_orchestrator.py:~1714`
`"10, the project failed"` matches as a metric; year ranges `"2020-2024"` match the range pattern.

**#14 — Only 2 date formats checked** `cv_orchestrator.py:~2291`
`"Mon YYYY"` and `"MM/YYYY"` — misses ISO dates, full month names, en-dash ranges.
